metadata.title: Adversarial Perturbation Detector: FGSM in One Epsilon

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The FGSM line x_adv equals x plus epsilon times the sign of the gradient — using only the direction, not the magnitude — flipping a golden retriever into an ostrich at invisible epsilon point-zero-one is exactly the attack I want to reproduce and probe. Help me (1) run FGSM on a pretrained ResNet-50 and print the top-three classes and confidence before and after, (2) sweep epsilon from point-zero-zero-one to point-zero-five and plot where the confidence flips, and (3) test whether adversarial training or input smoothing raises the epsilon needed to fool it.

props.command: The FGSM line x_adv equals x plus epsilon times the sign of the gradient — using only the direction, not the magnitude — flipping a golden retriever into an ostrich at invisible epsilon point-zero-one is exactly the attack I want to reproduce and probe. Help me (1) run FGSM on a pretrained ResNet-50 and print the top-three classes and confidence before and after, (2) sweep epsilon from point-zero-zero-one to point-zero-five and plot where the confidence flips, and (3) test whether adversarial training or input smoothing raises the epsilon needed to fool it.
props.segment: Adversarial Perturbation Detector: FGSM in One

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: A ResNet-50 trained on ImageNet classifies a golden retriever at ninety-eight percent confidence. Add a perturbation no human eye can detect — pixel noise bounded by epsilon equals point-zero-one — and the same model outputs ostrich at ninety-four percent. The image is unchanged to you. The gradient changed the model's decision surface. Goodfellow's Fast Gradient Sign Method from twenty-fourteen takes one forward pass and one backward pass and breaks any undefended classifier. This video builds the attack and measures what it costs.
beats.visual_intent: title card: 'epsilon=0.01 — invisible to humans, lethal to classifiers'; PROBLEM beat, no terminal

beats.narration_text: In the terminal: ask Claude to write a PyTorch script that loads a pretrained ResNet-50, runs FGSM with epsilon point-zero-one on a sample image, prints the top-three classes and confidence for both original and adversarial, and saves the amplified perturbation as a PNG.

props.command:
claude "Write a Python script using PyTorch + torchvision:
(1) load pretrained ResNet-50
(2) run FGSM with epsilon=0.01 on a sample image
(3) print top-3 classes + confidence:
    original vs adversarial
(4) save perturbation amplified 50x as PNG
Formula: x_adv = x + epsilon * sign(grad_x Loss)"

beats.role_note: terminal (Onda) — read the code before running
beats.narration_text: Read the attack line. x underscore adv equals x plus epsilon times x dot grad dot sign. That sign function is the key: it does not use the full gradient — it uses only which direction to push each pixel. Clipped to the image range. The entire attack is one line, and one backward pass through the loss is all the information it needs.

props.code:
# fgsm_attack.py  —  Claude Code output
import torch

def fgsm_attack(image, epsilon, data_grad):
    """Fast Gradient Sign Method (Goodfellow et al. 2014)"""
    # Collect the sign of the data gradient
    sign_data_grad = data_grad.sign()
    # Create the adversarial image: one step in gradient direction
    perturbed = image + epsilon * sign_data_grad
    # Clip to valid image range [0, 1]
    perturbed = torch.clamp(perturbed, 0, 1)
    return perturbed

# One forward pass + one backward pass = broken classifier
# x_adv = x + epsilon * sign(grad_x Loss)

beats.role_note: SLATE — PyTorch + real image required — human fill with screen-recording
beats.narration_text: Run it against a real image. You will see the top-three classifications before and after the perturbation, and the amplified noise map that the model responded to. The confidence flip happens at epsilon point-zero-one — invisible to the naked eye.
beats.visual_intent: HUMAN FILL: screen-recording of fgsm_attack.py running — show original image classification, adversarial classification confidence flip, and the amplified perturbation PNG side by side

beats.narration_text: Raise the stakes. Ask Claude to run an epsilon sweep from point-zero-zero-one to point-zero-five, record the top-one confidence on the adversarial class at each epsilon, and print a table showing where the attack crosses fifty percent confidence.

props.command:
claude "Update fgsm_attack.py:
-> sweep epsilon from 0.001 to 0.05 (10 steps)
-> at each epsilon: record adversarial top-1 confidence
-> print table: epsilon | adversarial_class | confidence
-> mark where confidence crosses 50%"

beats.narration_text: At epsilon equals point-zero-zero-five — noise bounded to half a percent of pixel range — the attack crosses fifty percent on an incorrect class. The model is not seeing the image you are seeing. It is seeing a mathematical structure in pixel space that you cannot perceive. Adversarial robustness is not a hardening question — it is a deployment context question. If your model is in a domain where inputs can be controlled by an adversary, the epsilon budget is your attack surface.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: epsilon sweep result — attack crossing 50% confidence; 'gradient sees what eyes do not'

beats.narration_text: Your move: run this script on your own model with your own images. Find the epsilon where the top-one class flips. Then ask: can an adversary control inputs to your system? If yes, that epsilon is your attack surface and you need adversarial training or input preprocessing before deployment.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) find your model's flip epsilon, 2) assess adversarial input access, 3) add adversarial training if needed

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. Your move: run this script on your own model with your own images. Find the epsilon where the top-one class flips. Then ask: can an adversary control inputs to your system? If yes, that epsilon is your attack surface and you need adversarial training or input preprocessing before deployment.
